We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Treasurer and other concerned offices coordinate with the suppliers and employees to inform them of the unclaimed payables, otherwise, provide certification to the Municipal Accounting Office that there is no valid claimants for the long outstanding payables to facilitate the appropriate reversion of funds;
The Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to reclassify the accounts that were erroneously recorded to the Other Payables account totaling P15.029 million to its correct account classification prescribed under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 on the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units; and
The Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to revert back to the unappropriated surplus the unpaid obligations/payables without existing supporting documents and no actual claim has been filed, other income received by the LGU and savings from completed projects totaling P4.888 million in order to present fairly the balance of the Other Payables account and Government Equity in the financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Municipal Development Council and the budget committee during the preparation of annual budget for 20% Development Fund to include only Programs/Projects/Activities which are feasible and could be accomplished within the target date of completion, after taking into consideration the priorities of the LGU leadership;
Strictly observe the schedule of implementation in accordance with Annual Investment Plan; and
The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), the Municipal Engineer, the Municipal Budget Officer and the Municipal Accountant closely monitor the implementation of the different development programs, projects and activities as reflected in the Annual Investment/Development Plan to avoid additional cost as a result of delayed implementation

We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the LDRRM Council to decide on the use of the unexpended balance of the LDRRMF, and review and update annually the LDRRMF Investment Plan to incorporate the PPAs to be funded from the unexpended QRF and DRRMF-MOOE of the previous years so that the Municipality becomes a disaster resilient community.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) instruct the Municipal Accountant to ensure that all claims for Fuel, Oil and Lubricants are supported with complete documents required under COA Circular No. 77-61 dated September 26, 1977, to enable the Audit Team to further validate the claims and to avoid the issuance of Notice of Suspension/Disallowance;
The Local Chief Executive (LCE) through the General Services Office shall implement control over the use of government vehicle through properly accomplished and duly approved and serially numbered Driver’s Trip Tickets, a summary of which shall be made at the end of each month in a Monthly Report of Official Travels for audit purposes; and
Monthly Report on Fuel Consumption and Monthly Report of Official Travels shall likewise be prepared and submitted to the Office of the Auditor for verification purposes to determine the reasonableness of the fuel consumed during the period.

We recommended and the Management agreed to the following:
Stop the charging of salaries and wages of Municipal Job Order employees against the 20% DF;
Revert to the 20% DF the amount of ₱1.097 million representing the inappropriate charges against the fund which shall form part of the unutilized balance of the 20% DF subject for re-appropriation to other allowable PPAs; and
Ensure that only authorized/allowed expenditure items shall be charged against the 20% DF as enumerated under DOF-DILG JMC 2020-1 dated November 4, 2020.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended the following:
The Inventory Committee conduct physical count of PPE and come up with the government required form of the Report on the Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) in CY 2019 and every year thereafter and the same shall be reconciled with the books of accounts; and
Require the issuance of Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) for all procured or acquired properties which shall be used to report on the receipt of property and equipment for official use from the Designated Property Officer.

We recommended the following:
Comply strictly with the rules and regulations as provided in Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances;
Monitor strictly the liquidation of unliquidated cash advances; and
Before signing/approving the clearances of employees, management official should diligently check their respective records of accountability to avoid incurrence of unliquidated cash advance.

We recommended the following:
The Municipal Engineer to submit justification/explanation as to the non-completion of the project on the intended completion date.
Investigate the reason as to why the project was delayed.

We recommended that the management:
Strictly observed the schedule of implementation in accordance with the Annual Investment Plan;
Mobilize the concerned offices in the early planning and implementation of development projects to avoid incurring possible additional cost as a result of delayed implementation; and
Submit justifications for the non-implementation and delayed completion of the above-mentioned priority projects totaling P13.784 million.

We recommended the following:
That for the ensuing year, the municipality plan for 20% DF projects that reflect the priorities in the Annual Investment Program and other local development plans and yet at the same time are eligible under the JMC No. 2017-1 dated February 22, 2017 of DILG and DBM. Other projects not otherwise eligible should be funded from the regular budget of the municipality;
That the management submit explanation/justification as to why these disbursements were made despite prohibition under Section 3 of JMC 2017-1; and
That the programs/projects/activities presented in the annual investment plan be classified in accordance with the classification in Section 3 of JMC 2017-1.

We recommended that Management, upon authorization from Sanggunian Bayan, open a separate bank account in the General Fund for the 20% Development Fund.  Upon opening, the Municipal Treasurer and Accountant should be directed to transfer the balance of the 20% development fund amounting to P24.097 million.
We recommended that Municipal Accounting and Municipal Treasurer’s Offices remit all the taxes withheld intact within the prescribed period to avoid penalties/surcharges.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer strictly comply with the regulations on the submission of the DVs and their supporting documents and RCDs and their official receipts to the COA Audit Team to avoid delays in the verification and conduct of other audit-related activities.

We recommended that HRMO:
Instruct the 68 contract of service/JO personnel not yet validated to present themselves to COA to establish their identity and existence;
Require the preparation and submission of monthly individual accomplishment report and assess the periodic performance of JO personnel to ensure value for money and avoid wastage of government funds; and
Strictly comply with the guidelines on formulating contract of service and job orders prescribe by the CSC to be consistent with other related policies and the constitutional mandate of merit and fitness in the government.

We recommended the following:
The Local Chief Executive directs all the department heads concerned to submit their action plans on the CY 2018 AAR findings and recommendations including that of the prior years, related/connected to their departments/offices and submit the same to the COA Auditor; and
The Municipality of Libacao shall faithfully observe the 60-day period within which to submit to the COA from receipt of the succeeding Annual Audit Reports, either in printed form or by way of electronic document, a status report on the actions taken on said audit findings and recommendations.

coordinate with the DILG Field Operation Officer in the generation and processing of sex-disaggregated data and GAD-related information.

Maintain adequate equipment and stocks to be used in case of emergency and an inventory report thereon shall be regularly prepared.

We recommended the following:
Limit the appropriation of discretionary fund to the prescribed 2% of the total real property tax collections for the next preceding year in conformity with Section 325(h) of R.A. 7160;
Refrain from appropriating separate fund for the same purpose such as miscellaneous expense; and
Exercise prudence in the utilization of the discretionary fund.  Ensure that this is use only for the purpose for which it is established and refrain from charging other expenses which should be covered by separate appropriation.

We recommended that the municipality comply strictly with the rules and regulations as provided in Section 89 of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 97-002 dated February 10, 1997 in the grant, utilization and liquidation of cash advances.  Monitor strictly the liquidation of unliquidated cash advances.

We recommended that the following:
The amount totaling P20,000 should be refunded/returned to SEF, and
The Local School Board thoroughly review the new JC and apply the new guidelines in planning, budgeting and disbursing of SEF.

We recommended that payments on infrastructure projects shall be supported with the required documents in accordance with Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012. We also recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) ensure the completeness of bidding documents necessary for the conduct of bid evaluation in conformity with the prescribed procurement procedures set forth under R.A. 9184 and its Revised IRR.

We recommended that for the ensuing year, the municipality plan for 20% DF projects that reflect the priorities in the Annual Investment Program and other local development plans and yet at the same time are eligible under the JMC No. 2011-1 dated April 13, 2011 of DILG and DBM. Other projects not otherwise eligible should be funded from the regular budget of the municipality.
We also recommended that the management submit explanation/justification as to why these disbursements were made despite prohibition under Section 4 of JMC 2011-1.
We recommended that the management take the necessary steps to make the project operational and submit explanation as to the cause/reason for its being non-operational.

We recommended the following:
The Municipality’s Gender and Development Focal Point System should be reorganized to conform to the structure and composition as required under PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.
The general functions of the GFPS be enumerated in its subsequent reorganization and make sure that the Executive Committees and the Technical Working Group are efficient in the performance of their functions, roles and responsibilities.

We recommended the following:
The municipality, through its Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator (MPDC), spearhead the setting up of GAD database and the maintenance thereof.  To set up the GAD Database, the municipality may:
utilize its existing database, planning and data-gathering tools and management information systems to generate gender statistics, age and sex-disaggregated data and other GAD-related information from primary and secondary sources of data such as surveys, studies, focus group discussion (FGD) results, progress reports, monitoring and evaluation results and ODA reports; and
coordinate with the DILG Field Operation Officer in the generation and processing of sex-disaggregated data and GAD-related information.
The GAD Focal Point System which includes Executive Committees and the technical working group provide technical assistance to the MPDC in ensuring that the database is operational at all times.

We recommended that the Municipality, through the Sangguniang Bayan, formulate or pass Gender and Development (GAD) Code.  Revisit the provisions of Ordinance No. 005-14 and make amendment(s) or revision(s), whichever is applicable, in order to come up with a feasible for approval GAD Code.  In doing so, a collaboration should be made with the LGU offices/ departments, national government agencies with local presence, women’s organizations and other civil society groups.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor issue an Executive Order creating the Monitoring and Evaluation Team to perform such functions as enumerated under Section 5 of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

We recommended that the Municipality:
Comply with the provisions of PCW/DILG/NEDA/DBM Joint Circular No. 2013-1 particularly on the procedures to be followed in the formulation, development and submission of GAD Plan and Budget in order to mainstream gender perspectives in their policies, programs, projects and activities.  A copy of GPB should be submitted to the Provincial Planning and Development Office (PPDO).
Prepare the Annual GAD Accomplishment Report so that the implementation of GAD programs, projects and activities could be monitored and evaluated.  A copy of the said accomplishment report should be submitted to the DILG Regional Office, through the DILG Field Operations Officer.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer implement and closely monitor the implementation of all the municipal projects to make sure that they are implemented as budgeted or planned in a given year.  He should likewise submit justification/reasons for its non-implementation.

We recommended that the SWM Board:
Revise and submit the 10-Year SWM Plan to the NSWMC as required by the National Solid Waste Management Council.
Regularly review and update the SWM Plan to ensure sustainability, viability, effectiveness and relevance in relation to local developments in the fields of solid waste management.
Prepare an annual SWM Plan for CY 2017 and every year thereafter, in line with the 10-Year SWM Plan, which will reflect the detailed PAPs and provide budgetary requirements thereof.
Efficiently implement and closely monitor the implementation of the programs, projects and activities of the SWM to make sure that they are implemented as budgeted or planned in a given year.

We recommended that the members of the MSWM Board:
Be mindful of their duties and responsibilities to ensure the sustainability and achievement of the goals of ecological solid waste management program within the municipality.  Refrain from delegating their duties and responsibilities to a single person; and
Regularly convene to enable effective planning and implementation of the programs, activities and projects of the SWM of the municipality.
